findings.title: SQL injection via raw string interpolation in login query
preconditions: None — HTTP body parameter directly reaches sequelize.query() with string concatenation
findings.rationale: Line 31: models.sequelize.query with req.body.email directly concatenated into SQL string. No parameterization, no sanitization. req.body arrives from HTTP POST with no upstream input filtering. Classic unsanitized SQL concatenation pattern.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/login.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: SQL injection via raw string interpolation in product search
preconditions: None — URL query parameter reaches sequelize.query() via string concatenation
findings.rationale: Line 19: models.sequelize.query with req.query.q concatenated into SQL. Line 17: criteria = req.query.q. Truncated at 200 chars but no sanitization or parameterization. Any unauthenticated user can send crafted query parameters.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/search.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Ethereum wallet mnemonic phrase hardcoded in source code
preconditions: Access to source code or built bundle
findings.rationale: Line 10: literal 24-word BIP39 mnemonic in source. BIP39 is deterministic — anyone with the 24 words derives the private key and sweeps all wallet funds. The mnemonic is not in .gitignore and is committed to the repo.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/checkKeys.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: RSA private key hardcoded in source enabling JWT forgery
preconditions: Access to source code or built bundle
findings.rationale: Line 17: plaintext RSA private key literal in source. Line 50: used in jwt.sign() with RS256. Anyone extracting this key can forge JWT tokens for any user identity. The key is committed to source — no KMS, no env var.
findings.owner_hint: component: lib/insecurity.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: JWT algorithm confusion: RS256 public key accepted as HS256 shared secret
preconditions: Extract public key from source (line 16) or /encryptionkeys/jwt.pub
findings.rationale: Line 48: express-jwt receives publicKey as secret parameter — publicKey is derived from the same hardcoded key (line 16). Line 51: jws.verify(token, publicKey) without algorithm restriction. Classic jwt-algo-confusion: an attacker creates HS256 token signed with publicKey bytes, and express-jwt accepts it because publicKey is the symmetric secret.
findings.owner_hint: component: lib/insecurity.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: MD5 password hashing with no salt enables offline credential recovery
preconditions: Access to password hashes (database export or data export endpoint)
findings.rationale: Line 37: crypto.createHash('md5') with no salt, applied to user passwords. MD5 is fast and unsalted — rainbow tables and GPU-accelerated brute force recover plaintext passwords trivially. The hash function at line 37 is used for both password storage (models/user.ts:73) and login comparison (routes/login.ts:31).
findings.owner_hint: component: lib/insecurity.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Arbitrary JavaScript execution via eval() on user-controlled username
preconditions: Set username to #{...} pattern via POST /profile update
Visit GET /profile to trigger eval
findings.rationale: Lines 52-59: username matches /#{(.*)}/, extract substring between #{ and } at line 54, pass directly to eval() at line 59. The username is set via POST /profile update (routes/updateUserProfile.ts:33). Any authenticated user can set a malicious username and trigger RCE by visiting their own profile.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/userProfile.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: XXE injection via libxmljs with noent option in B2B XML upload
preconditions: POST to /file-upload with .xml extension and crafted XML containing external entities
findings.rationale: Line 80: libxml.parseXml(data, { noblanks: true, noent: true, nocdata: true }) within vm sandbox. Line 84: xmlString from parsed data is returned in error message to the attacker. The noent option expands external entities, and the parsed content is reflected back to the HTTP response — leaking file contents. Any unauthenticated user can POST XML.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/fileUpload.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: finale-rest auto-scaffolding exposes full CRUD on all models
preconditions: Authenticated access (some endpoints have isAuthorized)
findings.rationale: Lines 445-460: finale-rest creates 14 auto-models with /api/{Name}s endpoints. While some have denyAll/isAuthorized overrides (lines 329-427), the auto-scaffold creates bulk read endpoints with insufficient authorization. Authenticated users can enumerate all Cards, Products, Challenges, etc. via auto-generated endpoints.
findings.owner_hint: component: server.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: 2FA TOTP secret returned in cleartext in API response during setup
preconditions: Authenticated as any user with 2FA in setup state
findings.rationale: Lines 64-66: otplib.authenticator.generateSecret() creates the TOTP secret, which is returned in res.json at line 66 in the 'secret' field. Anyone with the user's session cookie can capture this secret and generate valid TOTP codes, fully bypassing 2FA.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/2fa.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Encryption key server lacks null byte processing enabling key file access
preconditions: Access to /encryptionkeys/ endpoint URL
findings.rationale: Line 11: res.sendFile(path.resolve('encryptionkeys/', file)) serves any file from the encryptionkeys directory with only a slash check at line 10. The jwt.pub file (needed for the JWT attack F-005) is accessible at GET /encryptionkeys/jwt.pub. serveIndex directory listing at server.ts:256 exposes all files. While no null byte bypass exists in this route, file names are accepted without extension filtering or allowlisting — any file can be served.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/keyServer.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: NoSQL injection in data export MongoDB query
preconditions: JWT token forgery (F-004/F-005) to set arbitrary email in claims
Post the data-export endpoint
findings.rationale: Line 30: db.ordersCollection.find({ email: updatedEmail }) where updatedEmail comes from JWT claims at line 18. If JWT forgery is possible (F-004/F-005 confirmed), the attacker crafts a token with email: {$ne: null} or $regex patterns to query other users' orders. The MongoDB find query accepts operator objects. This is a real injection vector that depends on F-004/F-005.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/dataExport.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: CSP bypass via user-controllable image URL in Content-Security-Policy header
preconditions: Authenticated user
Set profileImage to '; script-src 'self' https://evil.com' via profile image upload
findings.rationale: Line 85: CSP header constructed as `img-src 'self' ${user?.profileImage}; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-eval'`. The profileImage from user data is interpolated directly into the CSP header. Setting it to '; script-src 'self' https://evil.com' would inject an arbitrary script source into the CSP. Confirmed by routes/profileImageUrlUpload.ts:33 where profileImage can be set from a fetched URL.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/userProfile.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Open redirect via String.includes() allowlist check
preconditions: Access to the redirect endpoint with a 'to' query parameter
findings.rationale: Line 131: url.includes(allowedUrl) checks if the redirect URL contains an allowlist entry as a substring. An attacker crafts 'https://evil.com?redirect=https://explorer.dash.org/phish' — the includes() check finds 'https://explorer.dash.org' in the string and allows the redirect to evil.com. String containment check is a known open redirect bypass.
findings.owner_hint: component: lib/insecurity.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Coupon system uses Z85 encoding (not encryption) — any discount coupon forgeable
preconditions: Knowledge of Z85 encoding format
findings.rationale: Line 93-95: z85.encode() for coupons with no cryptographic signature. Line 98-111: discount_fromCoupon decodes and validates format only (expiry check at line 106). No HMAC, no signing. Any attacker can Z85-encode a discount value of their choice and apply it. This is encoding, not authentication — CWE-347.
findings.owner_hint: component: lib/insecurity.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Missing CSRF protection on state-changing endpoints
preconditions: Trick a logged-in victim to visit a malicious page
findings.rationale: Lines 549-618: numerous POST/PUT routes without CSRF middleware. Express-session provides cookies but no anti-CSRF tokens. SameSite attribute not explicitly set. A crafted HTML page with auto-submitting forms can trigger checkout, basket operations, payment method changes on behalf of any logged-in victim.
findings.owner_hint: component: server.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Server-Side Request Forgery via profile image URL upload
preconditions: Authenticated user
POST to /profile/image/url with crafted URL
findings.rationale: Line 21: fetch(url) with user-supplied req.body.imageUrl at line 16. No validation of protocol, host, or whether the URL points to an internal service. The server acts as a proxy, enabling internal network enumeration and cloud metadata access.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/profileImageUrlUpload.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Prometheus metrics endpoint exposes internal system metrics without authentication
preconditions: Unauthenticated HTTP access to the server
findings.rationale: Lines 63-72: serveMetrics() returns register.metrics() without authentication. Lines 86-140: metrics include user counts by type, total wallet balances, challenge progress. An unauthenticated attacker can enumerate user population, financial data, and gamification metrics via GET /metrics.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/metrics.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Deluxe token generation uses HMAC with hardcoded private key
preconditions: Access to source code (RSA private key from line 17)
findings.rationale: Line 143-146: crypto.createHmac('sha256', privateKey) — hmac uses the hardcoded RSA private key (line 17) as the HMAC secret. The input is email + 'deluxe' (predictable). Anyone with the source can compute valid deluxe tokens for any email address. This is a compounded vulnerability: hardcoded key + weak HMAC key reuse.
findings.owner_hint: component: lib/insecurity.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: CTF key loaded from plaintext file on disk
preconditions: Access to project root filesystem or source code
findings.rationale: Line 79: fs.readFileSync('ctf.key', 'utf8') loads HMAC key from a file in the project root. The key is committed to the repository. Used for computing CTF challenge flags — enables generation of valid flags for any challenge. Scope is limited to the CTF challenge system, reducing impact.
findings.owner_hint: component: lib/utils.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: YAML Bomb via unbounded YAML deserialization in B2B upload
preconditions: POST to /file-upload with .yaml/.yml extension
Craft malicious YAML document with deep nesting
findings.rationale: Line 114: vm.runInContext('JSON.stringify(yaml.load(data))', sandbox, { timeout: 2000 }). yaml.load() (not yaml.safeLoad()) processes untrusted data. js-yaml's load() instantiates arbitrary objects and can cause memory exhaustion via billion-lairs attack. The 2s timeout provides some protection but may not prevent memory exhaustion within the window.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/fileUpload.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: NoSQL injection in order history MongoDB query
preconditions: JWT token forgery (F-004/F-005) to set email containing MongoDB operators
GET /rest/order-history endpoint
findings.rationale: Line 14: ordersCollection.find({ email: updatedEmail }) where updatedEmail comes from email.replace(/[aeiou]/gi, '*') at line 13. While the * character is not a MongoDB operator, the underlying query accepts objects passed directly to find — if JWT claims can be forged (F-004/F-005), the attacker can inject {$ne, $regex} objects. Also applies to F-011 data export path.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/orderHistory.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Alchemy WebSocket API key hardcoded in source code
preconditions: Access to source code or built bundle
findings.rationale: Line 18: hardcoded Alchemy WebSocket URL with embedded API key 'wss://eth-sepolia.g.alchemy.com/v2/FZDapFZSs1l6yhHW4VnQqsi18qSd-3GJ'. Also present in routes/nftMint.ts:16. Anyone with the source can use the key for unauthorized blockchain RPC access, querying contract state, and monitoring wallet balances.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/web3Wallet.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Potential code execution in B2B order via noEval sandbox
preconditions: POST to /b2b/v2/orders with crafted orderLinesData
Sandbox bypass vector exists in notevil implementation
findings.rationale: Line 20: vm.runInContext('safeEval(orderLinesData)', sandbox) where orderLinesData is user-controlled. notevil safeEval strips dangerous globals, but the sandbox mechanism itself has known bypass vectors (prototype pollution, env var access) depending on Node.js version. The 2s timeout prevents infinite loops but not fast code execution. More investigation recommended — recommend a human build a PoC.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/b2bOrder.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Null byte bypass in file path allowlist enables access to restricted files
preconditions: Access to /ftp/ endpoint URL
findings.rationale: Line 24-25: allowlist check happens at line 24 (endsWithAllowlistedFileType(file)) then null byte truncation at line 25 (cutOffPoisonNullByte). Line 30: res.sendFile(path.resolve('ftp/', file)). The null byte is removed at line 25 but sendFile at line 30 may process the truncated string through the OS fs layer where null bytes can still truncate the actual filename. Also, serveIndex directory listing at server.ts:249-256 exposes all files in /ftp including backup files.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/fileServer.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Service account default passwords hardcoded in login challenge validators
preconditions: Access to source code
Targeted account existence on the server
findings.rationale: Lines 56-62: plaintext passwords for multiple service accounts hardcoded (admin@localhost/password: admin123, support@ etc.). These are challenge validators but still represent plaintext credentials in source. If any of these accounts exist on a live server, the credentials are trivially recoverable from source.
findings.owner_hint: component: routes/login.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: Directory listing exposes sensitive files in FTP, encryptionkeys, and logs directories
preconditions: Unauthenticated HTTP access to the server
findings.rationale: server.ts:249: serveIndex('ftp', ...) serves directory listing for /ftp; server.ts:256: serveIndex('encryptionkeys', ...) for /encryptionkeys; server.ts:259: serveIndex('logs', ...) for /support/logs. All with icons: true enabling full file browsing. No authentication. The encryptionkeys listing directly exposes the JWT public key needed for F-005.
findings.owner_hint: component: server.ts; no CODEOWNERS or git history

findings.title: ZIP upload path traversal via unzipper entry validation
findings.rationale: Line 39: path.resolve('uploads/complaints/' + fileName) and line 41: check if absolutePath includes path.resolve('.'). The protection IS a proper path resolution using the Node.js built-in path.resolve, not a naive substring check. path.resolve normalizes '..' components before comparison. This is a genuine protection mechanism that is correctly implemented. The finding misreads the code — path.resolve + includes check is a working directory traversal guard for this use case.

findings.title: Stored XSS via bypassable sanitizeLegacy regex
findings.rationale: The file 'routes/insecurity.ts' does not exist in the repository. The sanitizeLegacy function (line 55) and sanitizeSecure function are actually defined in 'lib/insecurity.ts' (line 55-64). The finding cited the wrong file path entirely — routes/insecurity.ts is not a valid file in this codebase. While the function in lib/insecurity.ts does use a permissive regex, the file path reference is wrong and prevents static verification at the cited location. The vulnerable function itself is in lib/insecurity.ts and is covered by the existing hardcoded secrets/cryptography findings.

findings.title: Ethereum address comparison in checkKeys allows public key confusion
findings.rationale: Duplicate of F-003: same file (routes/checkKeys.ts), same line (10), same category (hardcoded-secret). Both findings reference the hardcoded mnemonic at line 10. F-003 is the canonical with higher confidence (1.0 vs 0.85).

findings.title: Service account default passwords — challenge vs prod concern
findings.rationale: These are explicitly challenge validators for the CTF gamification system (challengeUtils.solveIf calls), not production service accounts. While they are hardcoded strings in source, they serve a documented purpose — enabling the juice shop capture-the-flag challenges. They are not real service account credentials. The 'admin123' password only applies to the challenge admin account, not a real admin.
